title: scikit-learn integration
text: RieszBooster is an sklearn.base.BaseEstimator. Anything in sklearn.model_selection works on it without adapter code: cross-fitting, hyperparameter search, pipelines, model cloning. There is intentionally no bespoke crossfit() function — the design rule is to lean on scikit-learn’s well-tested machinery rather than reinvent it.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Cross-fitting for downstream inference
Plug-in \(\hat\alpha\) in a one-step / TMLE / DML estimator must be out-of-fold to avoid bias from over-fitting. cross_val_predict does this for any sklearn estimator.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_predict
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 2000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=2000,
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=4,
)

alpha_oof = cross_val_predict(booster, df, cv=5)
alpha_oof.shape, alpha_oof[:5]

((2000,), array([-3.6196816, -1.6876634, -1.0861515, -1.0516312,  1.2505229],
      dtype=float32))





set.seed(0)
n  <- 2000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(-0.02 * x - x^2 + 4 * log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

# The cleanest cross-fit from R is to call the sklearn function via
# reticulate, passing the underlying Python estimator object. The R6 wrapper
# exposes it as `$py`.
sk_ms <- reticulate::import("sklearn.model_selection", convert = FALSE)
booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 2000L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 4L
)
alpha_oof <- as.numeric(reticulate::py_to_r(
  sk_ms$cross_val_predict(booster$py, reticulate::r_to_py(df), cv = 5L)
))
length(alpha_oof); head(alpha_oof)

[1] 2000


[1]  1.301584  6.205725  2.930262 -3.024208  1.465699 -1.293439





Each fold runs its own internal early-stopping split — there is nothing to thread between folds.

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
Hyperparameter search with GridSearchCV
Score is negative held-out Riesz loss (sklearn convention: higher is better), so GridSearchCV selects the configuration with the smallest loss without any sign-flipping on your end.

PythonR



from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

grid = GridSearchCV(
    RieszBooster(
        estimand=ATE(),
        n_estimators=300,
        early_stopping_rounds=20,
        validation_fraction=0.2,
    ),
    param_grid={"learning_rate": [0.03, 0.05, 0.1], "max_depth": [3, 4, 5]},
    cv=3,
    n_jobs=1,
).fit(df)

print("best params:", grid.best_params_)

best params: {'learning_rate': 0.05, 'max_depth': 3}

print(f"best score (neg Riesz loss): {grid.best_score_:.4f}")

best score (neg Riesz loss): 5.8153

best = grid.best_estimator_  # already a refitted RieszBooster
alpha_hat = best.predict(df)
alpha_hat[:5]

array([-2.770411 , -1.6573501, -1.064923 , -1.0326685,  1.2886328],
      dtype=float32)





sk_ms <- reticulate::import("sklearn.model_selection", convert = FALSE)
base  <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
grid <- sk_ms$GridSearchCV(
  base$py,
  param_grid = reticulate::dict(
    learning_rate = c(0.03, 0.05, 0.1),
    max_depth = list(3L, 4L, 5L)
  ),
  cv = 3L,
  n_jobs = 1L
)
grid$fit(reticulate::r_to_py(df))

GridSearchCV(cv=3,
             estimator=RieszBooster(early_stopping_rounds=20,
                                    estimand=Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'),
                                                      m=<function ATE.<locals>.m at 0x13e1afc40>,
                                                      extra_keys=(),
                                                      name='ATE',
                                                      factory_spec={'args': {'covariates': ['x'],
                                                                             'treatment': 'a'},
                                                                    'factory': 'ATE'}),
                                    n_estimators=300, validation_fraction=0.2),
             n_jobs=1,
             param_grid={'learning_rate': [0.03, 0.05, 0.1],
                         'max_depth': [3, 4, 5]})

cat("best params:\n"); print(reticulate::py_to_r(grid$best_params_))

best params:


$learning_rate
[1] 0.1

$max_depth
[1] 3

cat(sprintf("best score (neg Riesz loss): %.4f\n",
            reticulate::py_to_r(grid$best_score_)))

best score (neg Riesz loss): 6.8145

title: scikit-learn integration
text:
What composability buys you
Because RieszBooster is a regular BaseEstimator:

clone(booster) returns a fresh unfitted copy with the same hyperparameters — useful when you want to fit the same configuration on multiple data subsets.
Pipeline can wrap a feature-engineering step in front of the booster.
HalvingGridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, BayesSearchCV (skopt) all work without modification.
get_params() / set_params() introspect/mutate the configuration; this is what the search classes use under the hood.

The general principle: anywhere scikit-learn’s documentation says “any sklearn estimator,” RieszBooster slots in.

title: Quickstart — ATE in 30 lines
text:
Simulate
A binary treatment with covariate-driven assignment and known propensity \(\pi(x)\). We will recover \(\alpha_0(a, x) = a/\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\pi(x))\) without ever estimating \(\pi\).

PythonR



import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 4000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02 * x - x**2 + 4 * np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})
df.head()

Fit
RieszBooster is an sklearn-style estimator: configure on the constructor (the estimand owns its input schema, so no feature_keys= repeated on every call), then .fit(df). Early stopping uses an internal validation split sized by validation_fraction.

PythonR



booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(treatment="a", covariates=("x",)),
    n_estimators=2000,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=4,
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
)
booster.fit(df)

section: Predict and compare to the closed-form representer
text:
Predict and compare to the closed-form representer
This is a synthetic example, so we have access to \(\alpha_0\) and can score the fit directly.

PythonR



alpha_hat  = booster.predict(df)
true_alpha = a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
print(f"corr(α̂, α₀) = {np.corrcoef(alpha_hat, true_alpha)[0, 1]:.3f}")

corr(α̂, α₀) = 0.934

print(f"RMSE         = {np.sqrt(np.mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)**2)):.3f}")

RMSE         = 0.973





alpha_hat  <- booster$predict(df)
true_alpha <- a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
cat(sprintf("corr(alpha_hat, alpha_0) = %.3f\n",
            cor(alpha_hat, true_alpha)))

corr(alpha_hat, alpha_0) = 0.962

cat(sprintf("RMSE                     = %.3f\n",
            sqrt(mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)^2))))

RMSE                     = 0.768

title: Install
text:
R wrapper (optional)
The R package is a thin R6 mirror around the Python implementation via reticulate. It does not require a separate install of any algorithmic dependency — everything runs in the Python venv.
Three steps the first time you use it:

Install the R package dependencies (reticulate, R6):
install.packages(c("reticulate", "R6"))
Either install the R wrapper as a package or dev-load it from the source tree. For this guide we dev-load:
pkgload::load_all("r/rieszboost")
Point reticulate at the Python venv where you installed rieszboost:
use_python_rieszboost(".venv/bin/python")
You can also set the env var RETICULATE_PYTHON before R starts:
export RETICULATE_PYTHON=/path/to/rieszboost/.venv/bin/python

After that, every R example in this guide runs verbatim.






NoteWhat R covers



The R wrapper exports every public Python class except the symbolic tracer for custom m() (see estimands and r-interface). All built-in estimands, both losses, both backends, and the entire RieszBooster API are first-class in R.

title: Estimands
text:
An estimand in rieszboost is a self-contained description of the linear functional you want to fit. It carries (a) the column names \(\alpha\) is indexed by, (b) any per-row payload columns \(m\) needs (e.g. pre-sampled treatments for stochastic interventions), and (c) the opaque m(z, alpha) callable itself. The RieszBooster reads this off the estimand object — there’s no separate feature_keys= argument.
This page covers the six built-in factories and how to write your own.

title: Estimands
text:
Built-in factories



Factory
\(m(z, \alpha)\)
Notes




ATE(treatment, covariates)
\(\alpha(1, x) - \alpha(0, x)\)
Average treatment effect — see examples/lee_schuler/binary_dgp.py


ATT(treatment, covariates)
\(a \cdot (\alpha(1, x) - \alpha(0, x))\)
ATT partial parameter. Full ATT divides by \(P(A=1)\) — combine with a delta-method EIF downstream. See examples/lee_schuler/binary_dgp.py.


TSM(level, treatment, covariates)
\(\alpha(\text{level}, x)\)
Treatment-specific mean — see examples/tsm.py


AdditiveShift(delta, ...)
\(\alpha(a + \delta, x) - \alpha(a, x)\)
Continuous-treatment shift effect — see examples/lee_schuler/continuous_dgp.py


LocalShift(delta, threshold, ...)
\(\mathbf{1}\{a < \text{threshold}\} \cdot (\alpha(a + \delta, x) - \alpha(a, x))\)
LASE partial parameter; same caveat as ATT. See examples/lee_schuler/continuous_dgp.py.


StochasticIntervention(samples_key, ...)
\(\frac{1}{K} \sum_k \alpha(a'_k, x)\)
IPSI via Monte Carlo over the intervention density — see examples/stochastic_intervention.py



Same call sites in both languages — kwargs match.

PythonR



from rieszboost import (
    ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention,
)
ATE(treatment="a", covariates=("x",)).name

'ATE'

TSM(level=1, treatment="a", covariates=("x",)).name

'TSM(level=1)'

LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7).name

'LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7)'





ATE(treatment = "a", covariates = "x")

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function ATE.<locals>.m at 0x7fd96696ec00>, extra_keys=(), name='ATE', factory_spec={'factory': 'ATE', 'args': {'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

TSM(level = 1, treatment = "a", covariates = "x")

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function TSM.<locals>.m at 0x7fd96696eb60>, extra_keys=(), name='TSM(level=1.0)', factory_spec={'factory': 'TSM', 'args': {'level': 1.0, 'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

LocalShift(delta = 0.3, threshold = 0.7)

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function LocalShift.<locals>.m at 0x7fd9669993a0>, extra_keys=(), name='LocalShift(delta=0.3, threshold=0.7)', factory_spec={'factory': 'LocalShift', 'args': {'delta': 0.3, 'threshold': 0.7, 'treatment': 'a', 'covariates': ['x']}})
 signature: (z, alpha)

title: Estimands
text:
Stochastic intervention: pre-sample, then fit
StochasticIntervention is the IPSI workhorse. It needs a per-row list of treatment values drawn from your intervention density. Build that column once, before fitting.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, StochasticIntervention

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
a = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a, "x": x})

# Modified-treatment policy: a' ~ Normal(a + 0.1, 0.2), 20 samples per row
df["shift_samples"] = [
    rng.normal(a_i + 0.1, 0.2, size=20) for a_i in df["a"]
]

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=StochasticIntervention(samples_key="shift_samples"),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
).fit(df)

booster.predict(df).mean()

np.float32(0.35250518)





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n); a <- runif(n)
df <- data.frame(a = a, x = x)

# list-column with 20 samples per row from Normal(a + 0.1, 0.2)
df$shift_samples <- lapply(df$a, function(a_i) rnorm(20, a_i + 0.1, 0.2))

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = StochasticIntervention(samples_key = "shift_samples"),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 4L
)
booster$fit(df)
mean(booster$predict(df))

[1] 0.3499112





The extra_keys field on the estimand declares which payload columns to pull through; in this case it’s ("shift_samples",). RieszBooster handles the wiring.

title: Estimands
text:
Custom estimands
If none of the built-ins fit your functional, write m(z, alpha) directly and wrap it in an Estimand. The library traces \(m\) symbolically to extract the linear-form structure.
The rule: \(m\) must be a finite linear combination of point evaluations of \(\alpha\), built only with +, -, scalar *, and calls to alpha(...). Anything outside that algebra (squaring, adding constants, integrals) raises immediately.






NotePython-only



Custom m() requires the symbolic tracer, which is currently Python-only. R users have two options: (1) use a built-in factory, or (2) define your m() in Python and call it from R via reticulate. See r-interface for the recipe.



from rieszboost import Estimand, RieszBooster
import numpy as np, pandas as pd

# A weighted contrast: 2x the ATE at level a=2 minus the level at a=1.
def m_weighted_contrast(z, alpha):
    return 2.0 * (alpha(a=2, x=z["x"]) - alpha(a=0, x=z["x"])) \
           - alpha(a=1, x=z["x"])

est = Estimand(
    feature_keys=("a", "x"),
    m=m_weighted_contrast,
    name="WeightedContrast",
)
print(est)

Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=<function m_weighted_contrast at 0x7fd955be9080>, extra_keys=(), name='WeightedContrast', factory_spec=None)


The estimand is now a first-class object you can pass to RieszBooster:

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "a": rng.choice([0, 1, 2], size=500).astype(float),
    "x": rng.uniform(0, 1, 500),
})

booster = RieszBooster(estimand=est, n_estimators=100, max_depth=3).fit(df)
booster.predict(df)[:5]

array([ 4.630866 , -2.446456 , -2.5141447, -4.992177 , -4.992177 ],
      dtype=float32)



What the tracer rejects
The error messages are designed to point you at the offending operation. A few examples:

from rieszboost import Estimand, RieszBooster, trace

def bad_squared(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) ** 2  # nonlinear in alpha

trace(Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=bad_squared), {"a": 0, "x": 0.5})

TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for ** or pow(): 'LinearForm' and 'int'



def bad_constant(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) + 1.0  # constant offset

trace(Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=bad_constant), {"a": 0, "x": 0.5})

TypeError: Cannot add a scalar to a LinearForm — m must be linear in alpha (no constant offsets).


If the operation you actually want is genuinely outside the linear-form algebra (a derivative or integral of \(\alpha\), say), you’ve left the regime the augmented-data approach can handle. The roadmap includes a slow path for general \(m\) via base-learner gradient boosting; today, restate the estimand if possible (e.g. Monte-Carlo-sample the intervention density and use StochasticIntervention).

title: Estimands
text:
Feature keys, extra keys
For reference, the Estimand dataclass has four fields:
@dataclass
class Estimand:
    feature_keys: tuple[str, ...]      # columns alpha is indexed by
    m: Callable[..., Any]              # the linear functional
    extra_keys: tuple[str, ...] = ()   # per-row payload columns (e.g. shift_samples)
    name: str = "custom"
    factory_spec: dict | None = None   # set by built-in factories so the estimand
                                       # round-trips through save / load
RieszBooster reads feature_keys + extra_keys off the estimand at fit time to construct the row dicts passed to m. Stick to that contract and the booster handles everything else.

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
Tracer, briefly
The user writes m(z, alpha) opaquely with +, -, scalar *, and calls to alpha(...). The library passes a Tracer in as alpha; each call returns a single-term LinearForm; the user’s m composes these via Python operator overloading. The result is a LinearForm whose .as_pairs() returns [(coef, point), ...] — exactly what build_augmented needs to construct the augmented dataset. Anything outside the linear-form algebra (squaring, integrals, constants) raises immediately at trace time, which keeps users on the rails of the fast path. See python/rieszboost/tracer.py for the full implementation (~130 lines, no external deps).

title: Developing rieszboost
Developing rieszboost
text:
Adding a new LossSpec
Implement the seven-method LossSpec protocol from losses.py. SquaredLoss (35 lines) is the simplest template; KLLoss (45 lines) shows how to handle a non-identity link with overflow protection.

Add the class to python/rieszboost/losses.py.
Export from __init__.py.
Add a one-liner R wrapper.
Add a runnable example to docs/backends-and-losses.qmd.
Test with pytest python/tests/test_bregman.py.

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
Adding a new backend
Implement the Backend protocol in python/rieszboost/backends/base.py — one method, fit_augmented(...), returning a FitResult whose predictor implements predict_eta and predict_alpha. XGBoostBackend and SklearnBackend are the two existing templates; the lightgbm backend is on the roadmap and will follow the xgboost template closely.
The backend doesn’t need to know about the estimand or the tracer — it sees a finished AugmentedDataset and a LossSpec and returns a fitted predictor.

title: Developing rieszboost
text:
Living-doc rule
Two related rules in CLAUDE.md:

README.md is the public-facing contract. If a change to the library makes any line of the README false, the change is not done until the README is fixed in the same edit.
The user guide under docs/ is the deeper reference. The same rule applies — public-API changes update the relevant .qmd page (in both the Python and R tabs where the page is bilingual) in the same commit.

This is enforced by the pre-commit hook at .githooks/pre-commit. Activate per clone:
git config core.hooksPath .githooks
The hook looks at staged files; if any of these are modified:

python/rieszboost/{__init__,estimator,estimand,augmentation,losses,diagnostics,tracer}.py
python/rieszboost/backends/*.py
r/rieszboost/R/*.R

then at least one of these must also be staged:

docs/*.qmd
docs/_quarto.yml
README.md

Otherwise the commit is rejected with a clear error pointing at the unmatched files.
For genuinely doc-irrelevant changes (internal refactor that doesn’t touch any signature, test additions, comment fixes), bypass with git commit --no-verify. That’s the explicit escape hatch — there’s no shame in using it when the situation calls for it.

title: Backends and losses
text: RieszBooster decouples three orthogonal concerns: the estimand (what functional you’re after), the loss (which Bregman-Riesz objective minimizes the right thing for that functional), and the backend (what does the actual gradient boosting). The defaults — XGBoostBackend + SquaredLoss — are right for almost every problem; this page covers when and how to swap them.

title: Backends and losses
text:
The default backend: XGBoostBackend
Data augmentation + xgboost custom objective. Each row \(z_i\) contributes (a) itself with quadratic coefficient \(a=1\), \(b=0\) and (b) a counterfactual augmented row at every point \(\alpha\) is evaluated at by \(m(z_i, \cdot)\), with quadratic coefficient \(a=0\) and linear coefficient \(b = -2 \cdot \text{coef}\). The Riesz loss is then a per-row weighted regression that xgboost handles via its custom objective interface.
Two knobs on the backend itself:
XGBoostBackend(hessian_floor=2.0, gradient_only=False)

hessian_floor — a lower bound on the per-row Hessian fed to xgboost. Counterfactual rows have \(H = 2a = 0\); without a floor, xgboost’s leaf-weight Newton step (-G/(H+λ)) is degenerate at those leaves. The default of 2.0 matches the natural Hessian on original rows. Almost never needs tuning.
gradient_only — short-circuit the second-order Hessian and use H = 1 everywhere (Lee-Schuler Algorithm 2 / Friedman 2001 first-order gradient boosting). Empirically worse than the default at matched hyperparameters; the second-order leaf weighting helps even though our Hessian is partly artificial. Reach for gradient_only=True only when reproducing the reference implementation byte-for-byte.

max_depth, reg_lambda, subsample, learning_rate, n_estimators, early_stopping_rounds are passed in from RieszBooster constructor — they’re standard xgboost knobs; see diagnostics and tuning for guidance.

title: Backends and losses
section: SklearnBackend: gradient boosting on any base learner
text:
SklearnBackend: gradient boosting on any base learner
If you want a non-tree base learner — kernel ridge, an MLP, a custom regressor — pass a base_learner_factory: a zero-arg callable that returns a fresh sklearn-compatible .fit(X, y)/.predict(X) regressor each round.
The backend implements first-order gradient boosting with line search: per round, fit the weak learner to the negative gradient of the Riesz loss, then closed-form solve for the optimal step size under a quadratic surrogate, then update the running prediction.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from sklearn.kernel_ridge import KernelRidge
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE, SklearnBackend

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    backend=SklearnBackend(lambda: KernelRidge(alpha=1.0, kernel="rbf", gamma=2.0)),
    n_estimators=80,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    early_stopping_rounds=10,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat  = booster.predict(df)
true_alpha = a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
print(f"corr = {np.corrcoef(alpha_hat, true_alpha)[0,1]:.3f}, "
      f"RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)**2)):.3f}")

corr = 0.913, RMSE = 1.663





set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

# Build the Python factory directly via reticulate so we get a fresh sklearn
# object each call. Passing an R function would also work — reticulate handles
# R-callable -> Python-callable conversion.
sk_kr <- reticulate::import("sklearn.kernel_ridge", convert = FALSE)
factory <- reticulate::py_func(function() sk_kr$KernelRidge(alpha = 1.0,
                                                             kernel = "rbf",
                                                             gamma = 2.0))

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  backend = SklearnBackend(base_learner_factory = factory),
  n_estimators = 80L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  early_stopping_rounds = 10L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
booster$fit(df)

alpha_hat  <- booster$predict(df)
true_alpha <- a / pi - (1 - a) / (1 - pi)
cat(sprintf("corr = %.3f, RMSE = %.3f\n",
            cor(alpha_hat, true_alpha),
            sqrt(mean((alpha_hat - true_alpha)^2))))

corr = 0.928, RMSE = 1.579





SklearnBackend is slower than XGBoostBackend (no native parallel tree splits) but the API surface is the same. It’s the right choice when (a) the data is genuinely better fit by something non-tree (e.g. smooth low-dimensional problems where kernel ridge dominates), or (b) you need to run inside an environment that can’t install xgboost.






TipWhen to skip xgboost entirely



SklearnBackend doesn’t import xgboost. If your environment is xgboost-hostile (alpine container, Python build with no OpenMP) you can ship rieszboost with SklearnBackend as the default by passing backend=SklearnBackend(...) to every RieszBooster and never installing xgboost. The library itself only lazy-imports xgboost.

title: Backends and losses
text:
Losses: SquaredLoss and KLLoss
The default SquaredLoss is the standard Lee-Schuler / Chernozhukov objective: \(\phi(t) = t^2\), identity link \(\eta = \alpha\). Per-row loss \(a \cdot \alpha^2 + b \cdot \alpha\). This is the right choice for almost every estimand.
KLLoss (\(\phi(t) = t \log t\), exp link \(\alpha = e^\eta\)) is for density-ratio targets — TSM, IPSI, anything where \(\alpha_0\) is a non-negative density ratio. The exponential link guarantees \(\hat\alpha > 0\) regardless of leaf updates, which can stabilize density-ratio fits where the squared loss would let \(\hat\alpha\) go negative through extrapolation.

PythonR



from rieszboost import KLLoss, TSM, RieszBooster

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=KLLoss(max_eta=50),
    n_estimators=300,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=3,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"min α̂ = {alpha_hat.min():.4f} (always ≥ 0 with KLLoss)")

min α̂ = 0.1174 (always ≥ 0 with KLLoss)

print(f"mean α̂ on treated rows = {alpha_hat[a == 1].mean():.3f}")

mean α̂ on treated rows = 1.845





booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = KLLoss(max_eta = 50),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("min alpha_hat = %.4f (always >= 0 with KLLoss)\n",
            min(alpha_hat)))

min alpha_hat = 0.1173 (always >= 0 with KLLoss)

cat(sprintf("mean alpha_hat on treated rows = %.3f\n",
            mean(alpha_hat[a == 1])))

mean alpha_hat on treated rows = 1.867





KLLoss requires all augmented b coefficients to be \(\le 0\) — equivalent to all \(m\)-coefficients being non-negative. Most density-ratio estimands satisfy this; it’s checked at fit time and raises with a clear error if you try KLLoss on (say) ATE.






WarningWhen NOT to use KLLoss



On the augmented dataset, pure-counterfactual rows (a=0, b<0) have no minimum in η under KL — leaf weights at low-overlap points can extrapolate. The squared loss is structurally more stable; reach for KL only when its positivity guarantee is genuinely needed.

title: Backends and losses
Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Tuning \(\hat\alpha\) is different from tuning a regression model. The Riesz loss has no held-out reference (you don’t observe \(\alpha_0\) on real data), so the validation curve from early_stopping_rounds is the signal during training. After the fit, .diagnose() surfaces failure modes — extreme tail values, near-positivity violations, single-point extrapolation — that often matter more for downstream variance than the bulk fit quality.
This page walks through .diagnose() on a deliberately under-tuned booster (so the warnings actually fire) and then maps each warning to the hyperparameter that fixes it.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Setup: a low-overlap dataset
We push the propensity close to 0 in part of the covariate space — exactly the regime where naive inverse-propensity weighting falls apart and where the booster’s tuning matters most.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 2000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
# Aggressive logit so π(x) ≈ 0 for small x
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8.0 * x - 5.0)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})
print(f"min π(x) = {pi.min():.3f}, fraction with π(x) < 0.05 = {(pi < 0.05).mean():.2f}")

min π(x) = 0.007, fraction with π(x) < 0.05 = 0.26





set.seed(0)
n  <- 2000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)
cat(sprintf("min pi(x) = %.3f, fraction with pi(x) < 0.05 = %.2f\n",
            min(pi), mean(pi < 0.05)))

min pi(x) = 0.007, fraction with pi(x) < 0.05 = 0.26

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
A deliberately greedy fit
Deep trees, no regularization, no early stopping — almost guaranteed to extrapolate badly in the low-π region.

PythonR



greedy = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=300,
    learning_rate=0.3,    # too high
    max_depth=8,          # too deep
    reg_lambda=0.0,       # no L2
).fit(df)

print(greedy.diagnose(df).summary())

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 2.4655
  mean            : -0.1415
  min / max       : -11.5966 / 19.8519
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1422
     0.90: 2.6658
     0.99: 10.2367
     1.00: 19.8519
  extreme rows    : 0/2000 (0.00%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -66.7209





greedy <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  learning_rate = 0.3,
  max_depth = 8L,
  reg_lambda = 0.0
)
greedy$fit(df)
cat(greedy$diagnose(df)$summary)

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 2.3614
  mean            : -0.2094
  min / max       : -13.6254 / 17.4702
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1424
     0.90: 2.6994
     0.99: 9.7480
     1.00: 17.4702
  extreme rows    : 0/2000 (0.00%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -62.8998

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Reading the report
The summary has three parts.
Magnitude block. RMS, mean, min/max, and |alpha| quantiles. The 99th percentile and the max are the load-bearing numbers. Healthy fits typically have max \(\le\) a few times the 99th percentile. Max far above the 99th percentile is the signature of a single-point extrapolation outlier — one leaf in the boosted forest is far from any training data and the linear extrapolation has run away.
Extreme rows. Rows where \(|\hat\alpha|\) exceeds extreme_threshold (default 30). The downstream IF estimator’s variance is dominated by the largest few weights, so even 1% of rows over the threshold is enough to inflate confidence intervals.
Warnings block. Two automatic checks fire when .diagnose() thinks something is wrong:

“X% of rows have \(|\hat\alpha| >\) threshold — possible near-positivity violation.” Either the data has genuine near-positivity (in which case truncate extreme_threshold lower and report the loss-of-overlap honestly) or the booster is over-fitting (tune as below).
“max \(|\hat\alpha|\) is >10× the 99th percentile — likely a single extrapolation outlier.” The booster found a leaf with no nearby training data and extrapolated. Tighten trees, increase reg_lambda, or stop earlier.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
A tuned fit
Same data, sane hyperparameters, with early stopping on a held-out split.

PythonR



tuned = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=2000,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=3,
    reg_lambda=1.0,
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)

print(f"stopped at iter {tuned.best_iteration_}, "
      f"best held-out Riesz loss = {tuned.best_score_:.4f}")

stopped at iter 1999, best held-out Riesz loss = -31.0978

print()
print(tuned.diagnose(df).summary())

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 3.2958
  mean            : 0.0136
  min / max       : -43.5758 / 30.7376
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1028
     0.90: 2.9091
     0.99: 14.2924
     1.00: 43.5758
  extreme rows    : 2/2000 (0.10%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -28.1668





tuned <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(),
  n_estimators = 2000L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L,
  reg_lambda = 1.0,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L,
  validation_fraction = 0.2
)
tuned$fit(df)
cat(sprintf("stopped at iter %d, best held-out Riesz loss = %.4f\n\n",
            reticulate::py_to_r(tuned$py$best_iteration_),
            reticulate::py_to_r(tuned$py$best_score_)))

stopped at iter 1999, best held-out Riesz loss = -29.3481

cat(tuned$diagnose(df)$summary)

Riesz representer diagnostics (n=2000):
  RMS magnitude   : 3.1136
  mean            : -0.0701
  min / max       : -15.5357 / 43.7492
  |alpha| quantiles:
     0.50: 1.1113
     0.90: 3.2547
     0.99: 10.1967
     1.00: 43.7492
  extreme rows    : 3/2000 (0.15%) with |alpha| > 30.0
  held-out Riesz  : -30.6263





The greedy fit’s max \(|\hat\alpha|\) shrinks by an order of magnitude, the extreme-row count drops, and the warnings clear (or at least become honest signals about the data rather than the fit).

title: Diagnostics and tuning
text:
Tuning recipe









Knob
Default
When to change
Why




max_depth
4
Drop to 2–3 if you see extrapolation outliers
Shallow trees can’t carve out a high-magnitude leaf for a single point.


learning_rate
0.05
Drop to 0.02 for harder problems
Smaller updates mean each tree contributes less; the ensemble has more trees to recover. Pair with higher n_estimators.


n_estimators + early_stopping_rounds
200, None
Always: set n_estimators generously (1000-2000), early_stopping_rounds=20
The validation split picks the right size for you. The default of 200 is for unit tests, not real fits.


validation_fraction
0.0
0.2 whenever you set early_stopping_rounds
Internal split for early stopping. (Or pass eval_set= explicitly.)


reg_lambda
1.0
Bump to 5–10 for low-overlap data
xgboost L2 on leaf weights — directly damps the magnitude of any single leaf.


subsample
1.0
Try 0.5–0.8 with very large \(n\)
Stochastic boosting; sometimes helps, often doesn’t matter for tabular Riesz problems.



For the backend itself, the most consequential knobs are exposed on XGBoostBackend:








Knob (on XGBoostBackend(...))
Default
When to change




gradient_only
False
True to match Lee-Schuler Algorithm 2 / Friedman 2001 exactly. Empirically worse than the default at matched hyperparameters; reach for it only when reproducing the reference paper.


hessian_floor
2.0
Almost never. Floors the per-row Hessian on counterfactual rows so leaf-weight Newton steps stay stable. The default matches the natural Hessian of original-data rows; lower it only with reason.



See backends and losses for the full backend story.

title: Diagnostics and tuning
section: A note on what .diagnose() is not
text:
A note on what .diagnose() is not
It does not estimate the closed-form \(\alpha_0\) to compare against — there isn’t one in general. It does not decide whether your sample size is enough. It is a sanity check on the fitted object and a smoke alarm for two specific failure modes (positivity, outlier extrapolation). For the actual quality of the fit, the held-out Riesz loss (best_score_) is the number to track during tuning, and the downstream estimator’s bootstrap CI is the number to report.

title: rieszboost
text:
rieszboost directly estimates the Riesz representer α₀ of a linear functional θ(P) = E[m(Z, g₀)] — the building block of one-step / TMLE / DML estimators in semiparametric inference — without ever deriving or inverting a propensity-style ratio.
It implements Lee & Schuler, RieszBoost (arXiv:2501.04871) with an sklearn-style API in Python and an R6 mirror in R. The R and Python interfaces produce bitwise-identical predictions; this guide presents every code example in both languages — pick whichever tab you prefer with the toggle on each page, and your choice persists site-wide.

title: rieszboost
text:
How to read this site
If you want to install the package and fit something in 30 lines, the README is the fastest path. This guide is the deeper reference:

Why and what — what’s a Riesz representer, why estimate it directly.
Install — Python install, optional R wrapper, dependencies.
Quickstart — end-to-end ATE in one page, executable, in both languages.
Built-in and custom estimands — ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention; how to write your own m(z, alpha).
scikit-learn integration — cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline, clone. (RieszBooster is a BaseEstimator.)
Diagnostics and tuning — what .diagnose() tells you and how to tune in response.
Backends and losses — swap to SklearnBackend for non-tree base learners; swap to KLLoss for density-ratio targets.
R interface — reticulate setup, parity guarantee, what’s currently Python-only.
Developing rieszboost — repo layout, tests, how to contribute.
References — papers cited throughout.







TipCode execution



Every code block in this guide ran when the site was built. The numbers and figures you see came from the version of rieszboost that produced this page — there are no stale snippets.

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
In semiparametric inference, you often want to estimate a functional of an unknown nuisance — something like
\[
\theta(P) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[m(Z, g_0)\right]
\]
where \(g_0(z) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z = z]\) is an outcome regression and \(m(z, g)\) is a linear functional of \(g\). Concrete examples:







Target
\(m(z, g)\)




Average treatment effect
\(g(1, x) - g(0, x)\)


Treatment-specific mean at \(a^\star\)
\(g(a^\star, x)\)


Additive shift effect \(a \mapsto a + \delta\)
\(g(a + \delta, x) - g(a, x)\)


Stochastic intervention \(a \sim h(\cdot \mid x)\)
\(\int g(a', x)\,h(a' \mid x)\,da'\)



A naive plug-in estimator \(\hat\theta = n^{-1} \sum_i m(Z_i, \hat g)\) is biased because \(\hat g\) is itself estimated. Modern semiparametric estimators (one-step, TMLE, DML) correct for that bias by adding a term involving the Riesz representer \(\alpha_0\) of \(m\):
\[
\hat\theta_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; n^{-1} \sum_i \Big[\,m(Z_i, \hat g) + \alpha_0(Z_i)\,\big(Y_i - \hat g(Z_i)\big)\,\Big].
\]

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
What is \(\alpha_0\)?
The Riesz representation theorem says: because \(m(z, \cdot)\) is linear in its second argument and bounded, there exists a function \(\alpha_0(z)\) such that
\[
\mathbb{E}[m(Z, g)] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha_0(Z) \cdot g(Z)\right]
\quad \text{for all } g.
\]
For ATE this is the inverse-propensity weight \(\alpha_0(a, x) = a/\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\pi(x))\). For TSM it’s the density ratio \(\mathbb{1}\{a = a^\star\}/\pi(a^\star \mid x)\). In general \(\alpha_0\) has a closed form involving the generalized propensity score — and that closed form is exactly what we want to avoid computing.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
section: Why not just estimate the propensity?
text:
Why not just estimate the propensity?
For ATE: estimate \(\hat\pi(x)\), plug in, get \(\hat\alpha(a, x) = a/\hat\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\hat\pi(x))\). Done.
This works fine when \(\hat\pi\) is well-behaved. It breaks when:

Positivity is borderline. If \(\hat\pi(x)\) approaches 0 or 1 anywhere in the data, \(\hat\alpha\) explodes. Truncation patches the symptom but introduces its own bias and is hard to defend.
The estimand is non-standard. For continuous shifts, stochastic interventions, longitudinal LMTPs, the closed-form \(\alpha_0\) involves derivatives of the conditional density of \(A\) given \(X\) — quantities that are themselves harder to estimate than \(\alpha_0\) itself. By the time you’ve written down the estimator, you’ve solved a harder problem than you needed to.
Misspecification compounds. Plug-in bias in \(\hat\pi\) propagates nonlinearly through the ratio \(1/\hat\pi\).
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
Riesz regression: estimate \(\alpha_0\) directly
Chernozhukov et al. (2021) observed that \(\alpha_0\) is the unique minimizer of the Riesz loss
\[
L(\alpha) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha(Z)^2 - 2\,m(Z, \alpha)\right]
\]
over all square-integrable \(\alpha\). (You can verify: the Gateaux derivative at \(\alpha_0\) vanishes by the Riesz identity.) So estimating \(\alpha_0\) becomes a regression problem with a custom loss — no propensity, no density derivative, no truncation.
The minimizer is well-defined whether or not the closed-form \(\alpha_0\) has nice tails. When \(\pi(x)\) is small at some \(x\), the Riesz loss penalizes large \(\alpha\) values quadratically; the resulting \(\hat\alpha\) is shrunk toward zero in low-overlap regions, which is exactly what you want for downstream variance control.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
Why boost it?
The Riesz loss is a regression loss like any other. Lee & Schuler (2025) show that gradient boosting is a particularly good fit:

Tabular-friendly. Boosting is the workhorse for tabular semiparametric problems; same hyperparameters that work for outcome models tend to transfer.
Direct gradient/Hessian access. For finite-point \(m\) (any \(m\) that’s a finite linear combination of point evaluations of \(\alpha\) — which covers ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, and Monte-Carlo’d stochastic interventions), the per-row gradient and Hessian have simple closed forms. xgboost’s custom objective consumes them directly with no surrogate.
Built-in regularization knobs. max_depth, learning_rate, early_stopping_rounds, reg_lambda all do exactly what they do for ordinary regression — and the tuning page walks through how to set them with \(\hat\alpha\)-specific diagnostics in mind.
crumbs: What's a Riesz representer, and why boost it?

title: What’s a Riesz representer, and why boost it?
text:
What rieszboost gives you
A scikit-learn-style estimator that:

Boosts \(\hat\alpha\) for any of the built-in estimands (ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift, LocalShift, StochasticIntervention) — or your own — without you ever writing down the closed-form Riesz representer.
Composes with cross_val_predict, GridSearchCV, Pipeline so cross-fitting and tuning are off-the-shelf.
Surfaces near-positivity and outlier-extrapolation warnings via .diagnose(X) so you can catch failure modes before they bias downstream estimators.
Has a parallel R6 wrapper in R so the same code runs in either language.

Next: install and the 30-line quickstart.

title: R interface
text:
The R wrapper is an R6 mirror of the Python RieszBooster. R-side and Python-side predictions are bitwise-identical on the same data because the R wrapper delegates every numerical step to Python via reticulate — the R object holds a reference to the underlying Python RieszBooster instance and forwards method calls.
This page is a focused R reference. The R API is covered throughout the rest of the guide via the bilingual tabsets — install in install.qmd, the standard fit pattern in quickstart.qmd, the full estimand inventory in estimands.qmd, and so on.

title: R interface
text:
One-time setup
After installing rieszboost into a Python venv (see install.qmd):
# Option A: env var, set before R starts
Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = "/path/to/rieszboost/.venv/bin/python")

# Option B: explicit, after R has started
use_python_rieszboost("/path/to/rieszboost/.venv/bin/python")
use_python_rieszboost() accepts either a path to a Python interpreter or a path to a virtualenv directory; it forwards to the appropriate reticulate function. It also imports the Python rieszboost module up front so any subsequent ATE(), RieszBooster$new(...), etc. work without per-call import overhead.

title: R interface
text:
API surface (R-side)
Every public Python class and function except the symbolic tracer is exposed in R:







R
Python equivalent




ATE(treatment, covariates)
rieszboost.ATE(treatment, covariates)


ATT(treatment, covariates)
rieszboost.ATT(treatment, covariates)


TSM(level, treatment, covariates)
rieszboost.TSM(level, treatment, covariates)


AdditiveShift(delta, treatment, covariates)
rieszboost.AdditiveShift(delta, treatment, covariates)


LocalShift(delta, threshold, treatment, covariates)
rieszboost.LocalShift(delta, threshold, ...)


StochasticIntervention(samples_key, treatment, covariates)
rieszboost.StochasticIntervention(...)


SquaredLoss()
rieszboost.SquaredLoss()


KLLoss(max_eta)
rieszboost.KLLoss(max_eta)


XGBoostBackend(hessian_floor, gradient_only)
rieszboost.XGBoostBackend(...)


SklearnBackend(base_learner_factory)
rieszboost.SklearnBackend(...)


RieszBooster$new(estimand, ...)
rieszboost.RieszBooster(estimand, ...)


  $fit(df, eval_set=)
.fit(X, eval_set=)


  $predict(df)
.predict(X)


  $score(df)
.score(X)


  $riesz_loss(df)
.riesz_loss(X)


  $diagnose(df)
.diagnose(X) (returns a list, with $summary as a string)


  $py
the underlying Python object (escape hatch)



R6’s $method() syntax invariably maps to Python’s .method(). Integer-typed kwargs (n_estimators, max_depth, random_state, early_stopping_rounds) need an L suffix in R, e.g. n_estimators = 200L. Other kwargs are auto-converted by reticulate.

title: R interface
text:
Verifying parity
The parity claim is not aspirational — it’s tested in CI. Here’s the same fit run from both languages, comparing predictions row-by-row.

PythonR



import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 500
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
a = rng.binomial(1, 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(2 * x - 1))))
df_py = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster_py = RieszBooster(estimand=ATE(), n_estimators=50, learning_rate=0.05,
                          max_depth=3, random_state=42).fit(df_py)
alpha_py = booster_py.predict(df_py)
alpha_py[:5]

array([ 1.4076698,  1.4764098,  1.6470399, -1.1931754,  1.1374395],
      dtype=float32)





set.seed(0)
n  <- 500
x  <- runif(n)
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, 1 / (1 + exp(-(2 * x - 1))))
df_r <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster_r <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE(), n_estimators = 50L, learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L, random_state = 42L
)
booster_r$fit(df_r)
alpha_r <- booster_r$predict(df_r)
head(alpha_r, 5)

[1]  1.212691 -1.263393  1.486148 -1.365574  1.212691





(The two RNGs are different — Python’s default_rng(0) and R’s set.seed(0) give different x and a columns, so alpha_py and alpha_r are not directly comparable here. To verify parity end-to-end, generate the data in one language, pass it to the other via reticulate, and compare predictions on the same df. The R package’s test suite includes exactly that test.)

title: R interface
text:
Calling Python-only features from R
The LinearForm tracer is Python-only — for now, custom estimands (those needing the tracer) require defining m() in Python. From R you can still drive a custom Python m() end-to-end via reticulate:
# Define a custom m in Python (e.g. in a script you `source` from R)
reticulate::py_run_string("
def m_my_thing(z, alpha):
    return 2.0 * (alpha(a=2, x=z['x']) - alpha(a=0, x=z['x']))

from rieszboost import Estimand
my_estimand = Estimand(feature_keys=('a', 'x'), m=m_my_thing, name='MyThing')
")

est <- reticulate::py$my_estimand
booster <- RieszBooster$new(estimand = est, n_estimators = 100L, max_depth = 3L)
booster$fit(df_r)
booster$predict(df_r)[1:5]
The same pattern works for custom LossSpec subclasses: define the class in Python, grab the instance via reticulate::py$..., pass to RieszBooster$new(loss = ...).

title: R interface
text:
Escape hatch
Every R6 method ultimately calls into the underlying Python object. If something isn’t yet wrapped, you can always reach through:
booster <- RieszBooster$new(estimand = ATE(), n_estimators = 50L)
booster$fit(df_r)

# These exist on the Python side:
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$best_iteration_)
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$best_score_)
reticulate::py_to_r(booster$py$base_score_)
The $py field is the underlying Python object; reticulate handles the conversion both ways. Use this for occasional access to attributes the R6 wrapper doesn’t expose; if you find yourself using it constantly, file an issue and we’ll add an R-side accessor.

title: R interface
text:
What’s not in R yet







Feature
Workaround




Custom m() for new estimands (the symbolic tracer is Python-only)
Define m() in Python via reticulate::py_run_string, then pass the resulting Estimand to RieszBooster$new(estimand=...). See above.


Custom LossSpec subclasses
Same pattern: subclass in Python via reticulate::py_run_string, pass via loss=.



Everything else — every built-in estimand, both backends, both losses, the full RieszBooster API including cross_val_predict via reticulate (see sklearn-integration.qmd) — is first-class in R.

title: Save and load
text: A fitted RieszBooster serializes to a directory containing the model binary plus a JSON metadata sidecar (loss spec, estimand identity, feature schema, base score, best iteration). Built-in estimands are reconstructed automatically on load; custom estimands have to be re-passed.

title: Save and load
text:
Round-trip

PythonR



import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tempfile, pathlib
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, ATE

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 800
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(-0.02*x - x**2 + 4*np.log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=ATE(),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=4,
    early_stopping_rounds=20, validation_fraction=0.2,
).fit(df)
preds_before = booster.predict(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    save_path = pathlib.Path(td) / "my_alpha"
    booster.save(save_path)
    print("Saved files:", sorted(p.name for p in save_path.iterdir()))

    loaded = RieszBooster.load(save_path)
    preds_after = loaded.predict(df)
    print(f"predictions match exactly: {np.array_equal(preds_before, preds_after)}")
    print(f"best_iteration preserved : {loaded.best_iteration_}")

Saved files: ['booster.ubj', 'metadata.json']
predictions match exactly: True
best_iteration preserved : 199





set.seed(0)
n  <- 800
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(-0.02 * x - x^2 + 4 * log(x + 0.3) + 1.5)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = ATE("a", "x"),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 4L,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20L, validation_fraction = 0.2
)
booster$fit(df)
preds_before <- booster$predict(df)

td <- tempfile()
booster$save(td)
cat("Saved files:", paste(list.files(td), collapse = ", "), "\n")

Saved files: booster.ubj, metadata.json 

loaded <- load_riesz_booster(td)
preds_after <- loaded$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("predictions match exactly: %s\n",
            isTRUE(all.equal(preds_before, preds_after))))

predictions match exactly: TRUE

unlink(td, recursive = TRUE)

title: Save and load
text:
Cross-language
Save in one language, load in the other. The on-disk format is the same — the xgboost model is xgboost’s native UBJSON, and the metadata sidecar is plain JSON.
# In R: save the booster
booster$save("alpha_ate")
# In Python: load the same directory and continue working
loaded = RieszBooster.load("alpha_ate")
loaded.predict(new_df)
The R parity test exercises both directions with tolerance = 1e-12, and our test suite asserts that predictions are bitwise-identical pre- and post-save.

title: Save and load
text:
Custom estimands
Custom (non-built-in) Estimands can’t be reconstructed from JSON — the user’s m() callable doesn’t survive a save/load cycle. The booster file itself is fine; you just have to re-pass the estimand on load:

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tempfile, pathlib
from rieszboost import RieszBooster, Estimand

# Set up data and a custom estimand (not a built-in)
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 200
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": rng.binomial(1, 0.5, n).astype(float),
                   "x": rng.uniform(0, 1, n)})

def m_custom(z, alpha):
    return alpha(a=1, x=z["x"]) - alpha(a=0, x=z["x"])

custom = Estimand(feature_keys=("a", "x"), m=m_custom, name="my_custom")
booster = RieszBooster(estimand=custom, n_estimators=20).fit(df)

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as td:
    p = pathlib.Path(td) / "custom"
    booster.save(p)

    # Loading without the original estimand raises:
    try:
        RieszBooster.load(p)
    except ValueError as e:
        print("expected error:", str(e)[:80], "...")

    # Pass the original estimand to reconstruct:
    loaded = RieszBooster.load(p, estimand=custom)
    print("loaded with custom estimand OK")

expected error: Saved booster at /var/folders/07/xf4cwwjd7nggpmy8y006ww6w0000gq/T/tmp5c_p3xms/cu ...
loaded with custom estimand OK

title: Save and load
text:
What’s in the file

booster.ubj (XGBoost backend) or predictor.joblib (sklearn-compatible backend).
metadata.json containing:

predictor_kind: "xgboost" or "sklearn".
loss: {"type": "SquaredLoss" | "KLLoss", "args": {...}} — re-instantiated with loss_from_spec(...) on load.
estimand_factory_spec: {"factory": "ATE" | ...} for built-ins, null for custom.
feature_keys / extra_keys, base_score, best_iteration, best_score.
hyperparameters — informational; lets get_params(deep=False) on the loaded booster mirror what you fit with.


The JSON is plain text and diffable; if the file format ever changes, rieszboost_format_version lets us migrate.
